coredata.dc:description: Utilizing nanoparticles in packaging materials has proven to be an effective method for enhancing the longevity and security of food products. Carbon dots (CDs) have a wide variety of potential applications in food packaging. A flexible film made of thermoplastic chitosan (TPC) and heteroatom-doped CDs has been created using a wet chemical blending technique. These CDs reinforced TPC based hybrid films exhibit flexibility, antioxidant properties, biodegradability, and resistance to UV radiation. The mechanical strength of the films was assessed through uniaxial tensile testing, while their low-temperature flexibility was evaluated by measuring the sub-ambient (∼-30°C) glass transition temperature (Tg). Additionally, the presence of CDs directly influences the permeability of oxygen and moisture, resulting in a reduction in comparison to neat chitosan films, a vital characteristic for packaging materials. Over a span of three weeks, the TPC-based hybrid films demonstrated a progressive weight loss (∼75 %), indicating their biodegradability. Furthermore, these films have shown promise as antibacterial and antioxidant materials. The durability of the films and the absence of chemical release from the embedded CDs within the TPC matrices were also verified, underscoring their considerable potential as antibacterial, biodegradable, and antioxidant packaging materials within the food industry.

(a) TEM image of CDs (b) size distribution histogram obtained from TEM dry diameter calculation by ImageJ (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum; (d) CDs emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths. (e) XPS survey scan (f) DLS size distribution data of the CDs.
Antioxidant effectiveness of CDs at various concentrations. (a) DPPH assay (b) ABTS assay (c) CDs' radical scavenging mechanisms.

(a) Wide angle X-ray diffraction of chitosan and TPC (b) FTIR spectra of chitosan and TPC (c) FTIR spectra of CDs and CDs loaded TPC films (d) Water absorption experiment of chitosan film and CDs loaded TPC films showing retention of moisture.

(a) UV–visible spectra of TPC based films (b) UVA blocking behavior of TPC based films (c) UVB blocking behavior of TPC based films (d) digital images of the TPC films (e) digital images of TPC films under 365 nm UV irradiation (f-h) Color parameters (L, a, b)) of different TPC based hybrid films.

shows the antioxidant activity of both unloaded and CDs-loaded composite films, as evaluated by (a) DPPH radicals and (b) the ABTS method. Antibacterial activity of the composite films specimens against (c) E. coli and (d) L. monocytogenes, respectively.

Cumulative CDs release behavior in different food simulants of (a) TPCD1 (b) TPCD2 (c) TPCD3. (d) Encapsulation efficiency of the TPCD films in different food simulant fluids (e) Cell proliferation results of TPCD specimens and comparing with control results. Error bars presented standard deviation (n = 3).

The rates of ethylene and carbon dioxide production were measured during the ripening and over-ripening of banana fruit held at a temperature of 20 °C and a relative humidity of 80–85 %. The mean value, along with its standard error, was calculated based on three measurements.

head.ce:title: Doped carbon dots reinforced flexible nanocomposite active polymer film for antibacterial and antioxidant activities

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Utilizing nanoparticles in packaging materials has proven to be an effective method for enhancing the longevity and security of food products. Carbon dots (CDs) have a wide variety of potential applications in food packaging. A flexible film made of thermoplastic chitosan (TPC) and heteroatom-doped CDs has been created using a wet chemical blending technique. These CDs reinforced TPC based hybrid films exhibit flexibility, antioxidant properties, biodegradability, and resistance to UV radiation. The mechanical strength of the films was assessed through uniaxial tensile testing, while their low-temperature flexibility was evaluated by measuring the sub-ambient (∼-30°C) glass transition temperature (Tg). Additionally, the presence of CDs directly influences the permeability of oxygen and moisture, resulting in a reduction in comparison to neat chitosan films, a vital characteristic for packaging materials. Over a span of three weeks, the TPC-based hybrid films demonstrated a progressive weight loss (∼75 %), indicating their biodegradability. Furthermore, these films have shown promise as antibacterial and antioxidant materials. The durability of the films and the absence of chemical release from the embedded CDs within the TPC matrices were also verified, underscoring their considerable potential as antibacterial, biodegradable, and antioxidant packaging materials within the food industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of carbon nanomaterials that possess exceptional characteristics, including optical, electrical, and antibacterial capabilities. Based on their physical and chemical characteristics, these substances can be categorised into different groups, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and carbon nanodots [1,2]. To review, GQDs are carbon networks that are disc-shaped and have one layer, with sp2 bonding [3–5]. In contrast, GOQDs differ from GQDs in that they incorporate oxygen functional groups within their structure. CDs are characterised by an amorphous, semispherical or spherical carbon network with sp3 hybridization. Lastly, CQDs are graphitic in nature and have a semispherical or spherical carbon network with sp2 bonding [6]. The development of nanomaterials has revolutionized materials science, allowing for the fabrication of materials that possess exceptional properties with possible applications in diverse fields such as medicine, electronics, and environmental science[7–10]. Since the advent of CDs in 2004, researchers have done extensive investigations into the synthesis, characteristics, and applications of CDs. Furthermore, recent research have indicated that CDs possess robust antioxidant properties and exhibit mild antibacterial effects, in addition to their biological applications [11,12]. These characteristics make them appealing materials for investigating novel uses, such as in the field of packaging. The use of bio-based polymers such as polysaccharides, proteins, and polyacids is becoming more prevalent in the production of active packaging films. These bio-based polymers provide advantages over synthetic petroleum-based polymers in terms of safety, biodegradability, biocompatibility, eco-friendliness, and sustainability [13]. The demand for creating a binary nanocomposite film employing a blend of proteins and polysaccharides in order to manufacture packaging films with biopolymer-based functional properties is increasing [14–16]. A film that combines several elements is anticipated to use the benefits of each component while mitigating their drawbacks.

The increasing environmental consciousness in recent decades has stimulated the advancement of biodegradable materials derived from renewable resources, with the aim of substituting conventional non-biodegradable materials in many applications. Polysaccharides, including starches, have numerous benefits that make them suitable substitutes for synthetic polymers in the plastics industry. These advantages include their affordability, lack of toxicity, capacity to biodegrade, and widespread availability. Chitosan is a linear polysaccharide consisting of β-1,4-linked-dglucosamine and N-acetyl-d-glucosamine [17]. It can only be dissolved in an acidic solution. It is the sole polycationic polysaccharide found in nature [18,19]. Chitosan is a highly promising substance since it is readily available, non-toxic, biodegradable, biocompatible, and possesses antibacterial qualities [20]. Furthermore, due to its commendable ability to form films, chitosan has been employed as a packaging substance, particularly as edible films and coatings for fruits and vegetables, with the purpose of prolonging the durability of perishable food items [21–23]. Unfortunately, chitosan exhibits the drawback of inherent water interaction, which might lead to unforeseen alterations in its structure and functions. This is a widespread occurrence observed in nearly all biopolymers [24]. Furthermore, the chitosan films in their pure form exhibit a particularly high degree of brittleness and fragility [25]. The utilisation of chitosan as a packaging material is limited due to its inadequate mechanical qualities and excessive hygroscopic nature [26]. Utilising a physical blend is a straightforward yet effective technique for enhancing the characteristics of biopolymer films [27,28]. The combination of chitosan with low molecular weight compounds has generated significant interest in altering chitosan. Plasticizers, which are small molecules, are commonly used to enhance the flexibility of biopolymers. The phenomenon of plasticization is commonly attributed to various hypotheses, such as the gel theory, lubrication theory, and free volume theory [29].

Advancements in food preservation and safety have been made possible by the incorporation of antioxidant and antibacterial characteristics into materials used for food packaging. Antioxidants are essential because they prevent food from oxidizing, which can cause deterioration in nutritional value, discoloration, and spoilage. Similarly, antibacterial agents play a crucial role in preventing food deterioration and pathogenic microbes from multiplying. By integrating antioxidant and antibacterial properties into food packaging, the use of synthetic preservatives can be reduced and the shelf life of food products can be prolonged. This dual-action packaging also protects against oxidative damage and microbial contamination. This method addresses the demands of consumers seeking healthier food options with fewer artificial ingredients. Additionally, it offers more transparent labeling and enhances the safety and quality of packaged meals. Therefore, it is essential to understand the relationship between antioxidant and antibacterial properties in food packaging to develop innovative solutions that enhance food safety and extend shelf life. Thus, the aim for this work is to develop flexible antibacterial film for active packaging applications. For this, heteroatom doped fluorescent carbon dots have been prepared by single step pyrolysis method followed by wet blending and casting technique. Moreover, the films have been tested as antioxidant, mechanically robust, UV-blocking capable and sustained release platform. The films were also tested against fruit preservation in real time and their extensive storage behaviors.

Chitosan (medium molecular weight, 190–310 kDa; deacetylated 75 %+) and glycerol were procured from Merck, Germany. All reagents used here are of analytical grades. Hen egg white lysozyme hydrochloride (from TCI Pvt. Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) was used as received. Other chemicals were used as received and all are of analytical grades. Double distilled water (DW) was used for experimental works.

CDs were prepared according to the reported processes with modification[30,31]. CDs were synthesised using thermal coupling of PEI with L-arginine. 0.5 g of PEI was initially dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water. 0.16 g of L-arginine in a 10 mL solution was added to the PEI solution and stirred for 30 min. The solution was subsequently moved to a muffle furnace set at 180 °C. A sticky brownish substance was formed after the reaction. The solid mass was dissolved with distilled water. The supernatant was obtained after centrifuging at 12000 rpm for 40 min. The solution was then passed through a 0.22 µm pores membrane to eliminate any large particles followed by dialyzed (molecular weight cut-off: 500–1000 Da) and stored at 4 °C for further analysis.

Thermoplastic chitosan (TPC) is made by dispersing 3 g of a chitosan in 60 mL of distilled water containing 30 % (by weight) glycerol and then drying the resulting solution [32]. After that, we heated the suspension to 70 °C for 1 hour while continuously stirring. The final product of the reaction was a gelatinous, slimy substance throughout the entire liquid. TPC was used after pouring the mixed solution into Teflon petri dish and then peeling it off followed by drying it at 60 °C in a vacuum oven for 12 h.

The TPC based control film was made using a wet mixing and solution casting procedure [14,33]. At first, ∼0.5 g of TPC was dissolved in 20 mL of double-distilled water (DDW) at 85 °C. When all of the ingredients had homogenised (become transparent), they were combined and agitated for another hour at the same temperature. Then, the resulting slurry was poured onto Teflon petri dish and heated in a hot air oven set to 70 °C. For CD-based hybrid films, CDs were added to the TPC dispersion, and the mixture was stirred for 1 hour at about 85 °C to make it completely homogeneous. It was then poured into Teflon petri dish and dried in a hot air oven set to about 70 °C. After the Teflon petri dish was completely dry, all of the films (control and CD-loaded) were peeled off and put into a desiccator to keep them from absorbing any extra moisture. The CDs were mixed in a varied proportion from 1 % to 3 % with respect to the polymer content and designated as TPCD1 (1 % CDs loaded), TPCD2 (2 % CDs loaded), and TPCD3 (3 % CDs loaded).

The sample films were subjected to thermogravimetric analysis using a TGA-1 instrument (Mettler Toledo Instrument, Zurich, Switzerland). The analysis was conducted by heating the films from room temperature to 800 °C at a rate of 10 °C per minute, while maintaining a nitrogen atmosphere. Furthermore, TGA was employed to precisely measure the moisture content of the sample films. The films were initially heated to 120 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min and then held at this temperature for 60 min to achieve thermal equilibrium. The moisture content was determined by measuring the weight loss at the conclusion of the experiment.

The DSC experiments were conducted using a Q 2000 DSC (TA Instrument, New Castle, USA) that was equipped with a refrigerated cooling system. The specimens weighing approximately 10 mg were tightly sealed in aluminium hermetic containers with lids and subjected to heating in the DSC chamber. The temperature range varied from −80–180 °C, with a heating rate of 10 °C /minute. The purge gas employed was dry nitrogen, flowing at a rate of 50 mL/min. The DSC experiment was conducted in triplicate for each film.

The tensile samples were cut into rectangular shape, with 80 mm in length and 10 mm in width. The gauge length was kept at 30 mm while the crosshead speed was set to 5 mm/min. At least three replicates of each experiment were conducted. All the casted films have the thickness ∼0.72 mm.

Preweighted xerogel specimens (10 ×10 x 0.1 mm) were tested by being submerged in a 1.5 g/mL concentrated (2 mL in volume) lysozyme solution at phosphate buffer solution (pH 7). All trials were conducted at a constant 37 °C. After removing the samples from the solution and washing them with distilled water, they were weighed. In order to maintain a steady enzyme activity, the lysozyme solution was replaced at regular intervals.

DPPH (2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) was utilised to examine the free radical scavenging capacities of different CD concentrations[14,34,35]. This method involves the reduction of DPPH in an alcoholic solution in the presence of a hydrogen-donating antioxidant, leading to the nonradical form of DPPH. In the radical form, this molecule shows absorbance at 515 nm, which decreases after absorbing an electron or hydrogen radical from an antioxidant substance and thus evolves into a stable diamagnetic molecule[36]. The DPPH free radical inhibition activity was measured by monitoring the reduction in absorbance of DPPH caused by CDs. This transition is evident as a color change from deep violet to pale yellow. Various quantities of CDs were introduced into a 100 µM methanolic solution containing 1 mL of DPPH[35]. The mixtures were incubated in darkness for 30 minutes, and then the absorbance at 517 nm was measured by using Cary 60 UV-Vis Spectrophotometer. The DPPH free radical scavenging activity was assessed using the following method:
(1) % scavenging activity = (Ac−As)/Ac
Where Ac and As denote the absorbance of DPPH free radicals at 517 nm in the absence and presence of CDs.

The ABTS (2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) radical cation decolorization assay was employed to assess the free radical scavenging capability[37]. 5 mL of 7 mM ABTS solution and 88 µL of 140 mM potassium persulfate solution were combined to produce the ABTS solution with a final concentration of 2.45 mM. The radical cation solution was diluted until it reached an initial absorbance value of 0.7±0.05 at 734 nm after being stored in the dark for 16 hours[38,39]. The green dispersion was then treated with different concentrations of CDs, and the absorbance was measured. The ABTS radical inhibition activity was measured by monitoring the decrease in absorbance of ABTS solution (using Cary 60 UV-Vis Spectrophotometer) due to the presence of CDs. This transition is evident as the color shifts from green to colorless. The equation used to assess the antioxidant activity of ABTS was also measured according to Eq. (1) where Ac and As denote the absorbance at 734 nm in the absence and presence of CDs. All experiments were performed in triplicate

The antibacterial effectiveness of the polymer nanocomposite films were evaluated using the time dependent antibacterial assay against L. monocytogenes and E. coli as described by prior investigations[40]. Following the incubation of bacteria in sterile media, the cultures were diluted to a concentration of 105-106 CFU/mL. The film samples, weighing ∼ 180 mg each, were introduced into 20 mL of the broth solution contained in sterilized conical flasks. The flasks were then placed in an incubator (labForce, USA) at 37 °C and with moderate shaking. For comparison, a control flask with no film was also prepared. 100 μL of culture from each flask was pipetted and inoculated onto agar plates at different time periods. The decrease in bacterial colonies was then quantified as a percentage to assess the total number of viable bacterial colonies. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of ethylene and CO2 generation
ce:section.ce:para: At each ripening/storage interval, six banana fingers were chosen at random and removed from the clusters. Each replicate contained two banana fingers, which were placed in 2.5 L glass containers equipped with septa. The containers were sealed for duration of 1 hour at a temperature of 22°C ± 0.5 °C. 3 mL of specimens of the air in the above space were extracted using a 3 mL gas syringe. The quantification of ethylene and carbon dioxide was performed using gas chromatography (GC), following the methodology outlined by Ramírez-Sánchez et al. [41]. The GC was calibrated on a regular basis using standard gas mixtures containing 0.98 µL/L of ethylene and 1 g/L of CO2. Ethylene production is quantified in units of ng/kg/s, while respiration rate is measured in units of µg/kg/s.
The CDs were synthesized by a simple one step thermal coupling approach as shown in Fig. 1

The TEM analysis was used to analyze the shape and size distribution of the CDs. The TEM image ( Fig. 2a) showed that the CDs were evenly distributed and had a nearly spherical shape. The particles had a size distribution ranging from 1.6 to 7.2 nm, with an average diameter of around 3.1 nm (Fig. 2b). The optical properties of the CD samples were evaluated by ultraviolet (UV) and fluorescence spectroscopy methods. In Fig. 2(a), it was observed that the UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs exhibit absorption peaks centered at around 250 nm and 290 nm. Peak at 250 nm is corresponded to π -π* transition. The later peak can be attributed to the n-π* transition of the C═O bonds[15]. The fluorescence characteristics of the CDs were investigated, and it was observed that their emission behaviour varied depending on the excitation wavelength as shown in Fig. 2(b). The emission peak exhibited a red shift when the excitation wavelength was varied from 300 to 400 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 2(b). This property is noticed as a result of the CDs' diverse sizes and the distribution of the numerous surface states caused by the various functionalities present on the surface of the CDs [11,14,42]. The fluorescence intensity of CDs remains over 98 % of its initial value after being stored at 4 °C for 30 days (Figure S1a). To store the CDs solution for extended durations, it can be dried in a vacuum oven to obtain a powder form, which can then be redispersed in water before use. There was no significant decrease in the fluorescence signal. The photostability of CDs was examined by exposing them continuously to UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm for 120 min. The CDs emission was observed at various time points. No significant change was observed in the emission intensity of CDs even after 120 min of continuous UV irradiation as depicted in Figure S1b. The XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 2(e) displays three clear peaks at 283.9 eV, 399.6 eV, and 532.7 eV, corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. The C1s scan spectra (Figure S2) revealed two peaks at 284.6 eV and 285.3 eV, indicating the presence of C-C/C═C and C-N/C-O, respectively[43]. As depicted in the Figure S3, the N 1 s spectra of CDs can be fitted into peaks at 398.4 and 399.1 eV. These peaks correspond to the presence of C-N (398.4 eV) and N-H (399.2 eV) bonds[44]. The result confirms the presence of elements in the CDs. The hydrodynamic diameter of the CDs was determined to be around 11.3 nm, which was significantly larger than the diameter observed in the TEM data. The increased dimensions of the nanoparticles observed in the dynamic light scattering data could be attributed to the hydration effect surrounding the CDs (Fig. 2f). This phenomenon arises due to the hydrophilic properties and large surface area of the particles, which attract and bind polar molecules present in the surrounding liquid.

The study utilised DPPH radicals and ABTS radicals to evaluate the antioxidant properties of CDs. Exploring the antioxidant capacity of different substances often involves using the DPPH free radical[11]. Therefore, the antioxidant activity of CDs was evaluated using the DPPH free radical scavenging assessment. Using a DPPH radical is commonly used to evaluate antioxidative activity, demonstrating the changes in spectrophotometry caused by the interaction with an antioxidant. This method is known for its swiftness, ease, and affordability. DPPH is considered a stable free radical because of the nitrogen atoms with unpaired electrons and its positioning within a molecular structure composed of three benzene rings. The procedure of DPPH interacting with an antioxidant includes the acceptance of a hydrogen radical, leading to the formation of a DPPH–H complex. This transformation is evident by a change in color from deep violet to pale yellow[45]. To evaluate the decrease of the DPPH radical, the absorbance at 517 nm was measured compared to a blank sample. The study examined how the ability of CDs to scavenge DPPH radicals varied with different concentrations ranging from 0 to 600 μg/mL, as shown in Fig. 3(a). The study noticed a rise in DPPH scavenging activity when the concentration of CDs increased gradually up to 390 μg/mL. Further increases in CDs lead to a little change in the antioxidant effectiveness, reaching a saturation point at around 545 μg/mL. CDs exhibited concentration-dependent ABTS•+ scavenging capabilities, demonstrated by the color change from blue-green to colorless (Fig. 3b). It was found that the absorbance value decreased as the concentration of CDs increased. The findings of the ABTS•+ scavenging experiment showed a relationship with the amount of CDs. The scavenging activity increases as CDs concentrations raised up to 450 μg/mL, reaching a saturation point after that. Adding dopants may improve radical scavenging by aiding electron transfer to radicals, resulting in either proton transfer or the formation of an adduct[14]. Hydrogen transfer from the hydroxyl groups of CDs can reduce and quench the free radicals ABTS• and DPPH•. Fig. 3(c) depicts the complete portrayal of the radical scavenging activity of CDs.

Fig. 4(a) shows the XRD patterns of chitosan and TPC films. The characteristic peak of chitosan is found at 20.13° which is due to diffraction from (100) plane. After treating it with glycerol, the peak broadening was observed at the same position associated with a satellite peak at 28–36° which is actually the amorphous halo of the TPC. Glycerol affects the inter-chain friction and destroys the crystalline nature of chitosan. This affects increment in the interlayer spacing between the consecutive chitosan chains and H-bonding interactions. Moreover, a slight shift was also observed at ∼19° indicating influence of plasticizer molecules by some secondary interaction which is also explained by FTIR spectra.

Fig. 4(b,c) show the FTIR spectra of chitosan, TPC, CDs, and CDs reinforced TPC films. Broad peaks in the 3300–3500 cm−1 region correspond to O-H stretching vibrations, while peaks at 3200–3400 cm−1 signify N-H stretching vibrations. Aliphatic C-H stretching vibrations are evident around 2800–3000 cm−1. The presence of acetyl groups is indicated by a peak at 1650–1700 cm−1, representing CO stretching vibrations, though its intensity may vary based on the degree of deacetylation [17]. Peaks around 1600–1650 cm−1 and 1500–1550 cm−1 denote amide I and II bands, respectively, confirming the presence of amide bonds in chitosan. Additionally, peaks around 1300–1400 cm−1 indicate C-N stretching vibrations, while a complex series of peaks in the 1000–1200 cm−1 region corresponds to glycosidic bond stretching vibrations between glucosamine units. Similar to previous research, the TPC spectrum exhibited a band at 3300 cm−1 that overlaps the N-H stretching band in the same region, which can be attributed to -OH stretching [46]. An abbreviated peak at 1647 cm−1 is indicative of stretching of the C-O (amide I) group, while peaks at 1717 cm−1 and 1150–1014 cm−1 correspond to the stretching of the carbonyl, carboxyl (COO-), and -CH groups, respectively. Typically, a broad peak in the range of 3000–3600 cm−1 represents O-H stretching vibrations attributed to hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs. Peaks around 2800–3000 cm−1 signify C-H stretching vibrations, indicative of aliphatic and aromatic carbon functionalities. The presence of CO stretching vibrations from carbonyl groups can be observed around 1700–1750 cm−1. Additionally, peaks in the region of 1600–1650 cm−1 often represent CC stretching vibrations from aromatic carbon structures. Peaks around 1400–1500 cm−1 indicate the presence of C-N stretching vibrations, which might be associated with amine or amide groups [47,48]. In addition, the occurrence of peaks in the fingerprint region (below 1200 cm−1) can provide valuable information about the specific configurations of carbon-carbon and carbon-oxygen bonds. All the characteristic peaks are visible for both the TPC and CDs in the loaded TPC films, confirming the formation of a hybrid film. The FTIR spectra validate the successful integration of CDs into the TPC matrix, resulting in improved chemical interactions and structural integrity, which is essential for packaging uses.

One significant disadvantage of chitosan films is their inherent hygroscopic nature, which causes them to naturally interact with water. This interaction can result in undesirable alterations to both the structure and properties of the films. Therefore, it is crucial to accurately determine the moisture content present in polymer films. An investigation was conducted to determine the moisture absorption of the sample films that were exposed to a relative humidity of 60 %. Fig. 4(d) displays the moisture content of the sample neat and hybrid films. The chitosan film demonstrates moderate water adsorption activity at room temperature, with a water content of ∼7 %. The moisture content of the plasticizers varied depending on their nature when they were added. The weight percentage decreases from ∼11 wt% for the film plasticized with glycerol (TPC) to 8.5 % after addition of CDs into the TPC. These findings suggest that the water content is correlated with the chemical composition of the plasticizer and CDs. In general, it is anticipated that the terminal hydroxyl groups in plasticizer molecules will be more easily reached by water. The number of these terminal hydroxyl groups is inversely proportional to the molecular weight of the plasticizers. The lowering of water content with increasing the CDs could be implied to their better polar-polar interaction between TPC and surface functionalities of CDs. This helps the hybrid tighter and denser compared to the neat TPC film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of TPC hybrid films
ce:section.ce:para: Figure S4 (a) illustrates the stress-strain curves of the pure chitosan, glycerol plasticized chitosan and CDs loaded hybrid films at normal room temperature. Observations indicate that the chitosan film, when in a pure state, exhibits the highest tensile strength (TS). The pattern of behavior exhibited by brittle materials is consistent with this. Despite having a lower TS than chitosan film, glycerol plasticized chitosan film (TPC) demonstrates a higher elongation at break of approximately 95 %. Therefore, under uniaxial stretching, TPC film demonstrates elasticity similar to that of rubber. Here we can also see the CDs' reinforcing behavior. For TPCD1, there were no significant improvement in their TS and almost identical to unfilled TPC. The TS and elongation at break values of each specimens have been depicted in Figure S4 (b,c). But TPCD2 and TPCD3 show TS of ∼14 and ∼19 MPa respectively. This could imply the reinforcing nature of CDs. Normally TPC could anchor with nanoparticle surface due to polar-polar interaction. There are a number of reasons related to the special characteristics of CDs that explain why they improve the mechanical properties of polymer films. CDs have high surface area, so they can physically adsorb to the polymer macrochains, which strengthens the film. In addition, CDs can help the polymer matrix a more compact and uniform structure, which improves mechanical integrity. The mechanical strength of a polymer matrix can be increased by using CDs as nucleating agents, which encourage the formation of crystalline domains within the matrix [49]. Hence, TPC chains are tightly bound to the surface of CDs. Moreover, the CDs are forming physical crosslinking points in the TPC matrix. These physical junctions act as stress dissipation points which promote fast stress dissipation during performance and delay the rupture point. The addition of CDs enhances the mechanical strength, and performance. These findings introduce a novel approach to developing advanced packaging materials with improved properties, offering new insights into the potential applications of nanocomposite films in the food industry.

The UV absorbance and transmission spectra of the pure-TPC film and TPCD hybrid films with CDs were measured to assess the suitability of the fabricated multifunctional packaging films ( Fig. 5a-c). The TPC neat film exhibited a high level of transparency in the visible region, with a transmittance of 76.1 % at a wavelength of 660 nm. The pristine film was found to be unsuitable for effectively blocking UVA, as it only blocked approximately 66.4 ± 0.5 % of UVA light and approximately 88.9 ± 0.5 % of UVB light. By incorporating various concentrations of CDs into the TPC matrix, the films exhibited high absorption of both UVA and UVB radiation. The experimental results of UV absorbance, screening, and transmittance investigations confirm that the suggested films have an adequately high level of transparency to visible light and effectively prevent the majority of destructive UVA and UVB radiation, without the need for semiconducting nanoparticles.

The colour properties of films play a crucial role in the selection of a product for customers, depending on the specific substances and manufacturing methods used in the film production. The visual appearance of TPC films is significantly altered by the addition of various concentrations of CDs. Fig. 5(d, e) demonstrates that the TPC neat film was transparent. However, when CDs were added, the films became less transparent and acquired a light brown colour. This change in appearance was a result of the yellowish hue of the incorporated CDs. The hue of yellow intensified proportionally with the increase in CD concentration. The presence of a smooth and uniform texture indicates that the CDs are evenly distributed throughout the TPC matrix. Fig. 5(f-h) depict the mean colorimetric characteristics of the produced films. There was a noticeable contrast in the average brightness (L-values) between the TPC films without any additives and the TPC films loaded with CD. The TPC films without additives had the highest brightness, but when CD was added, the brightness gradually decreased. Additionally, the redness (a), yellowness (b), and total colour difference (ΔE) values increased. These changes were attributed to the yellowish colour of the CDs that caused a decrease in glow and total visual exterior look by producing opaqueness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gas barrier property, wettability and biodegradability
ce:section.ce:para: The physiological metabolism of fruits and vegetables is regulated by the gaseous compounds found in packing, which are primarily responsible for the majority of decay processes [50,51]. The extended preservation of fruits and vegetables can be attributed to the favourable gas environment, characterised by low levels of oxygen and high levels of carbon dioxide. This gas condition effectively minimises their respiratory impact and inhibits the proliferation of microorganisms [52]. Fig. 6a and (b) display the oxygen transmission rate (OTR) and water vapour transmission rate (WVTR) of TPC films containing pristine and CDs, respectively, as the CDs content varies. The OTR and WVTR of the TPC films without any fillers were approximately 64 cm3.μm/(m2.d.kPa) and the WVTR value was 874 g/m2.24 h, respectively. Following the inclusion of CDs, both the OTR and WVTR exhibited deterioration. The TPCD1 sample shows minimal alterations in both the OTR and WVTR when compared to the original TPC film. Nevertheless, the decrease in OTR and WVTR were more evident in the films produced with higher concentration of CDs in TPC, as illustrated in the accompanying figures. The OTR and WVTR of the TPCD3 specimen were measured to be approximately 38.7 cm3.μm/(m2.d.kPa) and 633.8 g/m2.24 h, respectively. Hybrid films displayed improved gas barrier characteristics as a result of their denser network structures, which impeded the polymer matrix's motion associated with the gaseous diffusion coefficient [53]. The gas permeability is strongly associated with the degree of interconnectivity within the TPC phase. CDs, as physical crosslinking systems, can potentially decrease gas permeability because of the dense polymer matrices. Several researchers reported OTR and WVTR of biopolymer based films. The anticipated oxygen permeability coefficient of the polylactic acid/nanocellulose composite was projected to be as low as 100 cm3 (STP) 100 µm.m−2d−1.bar−1 [54]. An investigation was conducted to assess the impact of varying concentrations of chitosan and zeolite microparticles on the water vapor permeability of composite films [55]. The WVTR increased proportionally with the concentration of chitosan and zeolite, as well as with the molecular weight (MW). The film produced from a 1.5 % (w/v) solution of chitosan (77 kDa) and 3.5 % (w/w) of zeolite exhibited the lowest WVP. A study investigated the effects of using alginate-based functional coatings containing sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) and grapefruit seed extract (GFSE) on the antioxidant and antibacterial properties of kraft paper used for food packaging [56]. The incorporation of SQDs into the coating formulation resulted in a notable augmentation in water absorption. Both the Paper/Alginate/SQD and Paper/Alginate/SQD/GFSE materials exhibited a water absorption rate of approximately 47 %, which can be attributed to the hydrophilic properties of SQDs. The WVTR was also studied for pectin/gelatin blend films where WVTR was increased with increasing the quantum dots concentration [57].

The water contact angle (WCA) was measured on the surface of TPC-based multifunctional films that contained CDs in order to assess the surface hydrophobicity of the films. Fig. 6(c) demonstrates that the WCA of TPC films rose as the concentration of CDs increased. Chitosan generally exhibits a WCA value of 96° when it is placed on a solid surface. This value indicates its moderate hydrophobicity, which is attributed to a combination of factors. The presence of hydroxyl and amino functional groups in its chemical structure enables it to engage in hydrogen bonding with water molecules. Nevertheless, the inclusion of hydrophobic constituents in the molecule contributes to its overall hydrophobic behaviour. Furthermore, the chitosan coatings typically exhibit a surface morphology that includes small-scale unevenness, which promotes the trapping of air beneath liquid droplets and enhances the contact angle [58]. The wetting behaviour of chitosan can be further influenced by factors such as the degree of deacetylation, substrate surface energy, and any additives or modifications. With increasing the CDs concentration the WCA values of the TPC hybrid films show gradual increment. These carbon-based materials at the nanoscale level add surface roughness to the TPC film, resulting in the formation of microstructures that capture air pockets and decrease the amount of contact between the solid and liquid. Moreover, carbon dots exhibit hydrophobic surface functional groups, which augment the film's overall hydrophobicity. By incorporating this substance, the polymer's surface energy is reduced, resulting in decreased susceptibility to water wetting [11]. In addition, the chemical interactions between carbon dots and the polymer matrix cause additional modifications to the surface properties, resulting in an elevation of water contact angle values and indicating a heightened hydrophobic surface behaviour.

The disposal of polymeric materials after they have been used is a persistent issue in society and poses a threat to the environment. Hence, it is imperative in academic fields to advocate for the utilisation of synthesised materials in practical contexts by conducting biodegradation assays on the hydrogels that are produced. The findings of the study on the degradation of hydrogel in CDs are illustrated in Fig. 6(d). The degradation rate was assessed at the initial time point and at 3, 7, and 21 days. The structure of lysozyme derived from chicken eggs bears resemblance to that of human lysozyme [59]. The degradation in the absence of lysozyme was also assessed by employing a phosphate buffer solution (PBS). Lysozyme, a glycoside hydrolase, assists in the digestion of polysaccharides by breaking the 1, 4-beta glycosidic link [60]. Based on the findings of the biodegradation study, the weight of hybrid films composed of chitosan consistently decreases over a period of time [59,61,62]. The hydrolysis of the polysaccharide segment results in the breaking of its backbone chain, producing a glucopyranose ring of sugar, which explains this occurrence [63]. The decrease in degradation rate is attributed to a reduction in the number of degradation sites [64]. The TPCD3 sample immersed in PBS has no noticeable change in weight reduction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and antibacterial activity of the CDs loaded composite TPC films
ce:section.ce:para: An assessment was conducted on the antioxidant properties of the CDs loaded hybrid films using DPPH and ABTS assay. The various antioxidant characteristics of films combined with CDs depend on the CD's concentration. CDs are recognized for their strong antioxidant properties, which are influenced by their source and preparation methods. During the ABTS and DPPH experiments, the TPC sample exhibited less than 3 % activity in scavenging free radicals. Upon the introduction of CDs, there was a noticeable rise in the DPPH and ABTS scavenging activity displayed by the composite films, as shown in Fig. 7a,b. The abundance of electron-donating functional groups on the surface of CDs is thought to contribute to their high antioxidant activity. These functionalities consist of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and other components. By incorporating CDs, a composite film was developed that exhibited enhanced antioxidant properties compared to pure TPC. This result suggests that the ability to scavenge free radicals was significantly boosted by the presence of CDs. Antioxidant materials are important in food packaging because they prevent the oxidation of food products, which can cause spoilage, off-flavours, nutritional value loss, and the formation of harmful compounds. Antioxidants enhance the shelf life of food by suppressing oxidation, ensuring its quality and safety. The as-synthesized CDs in food packaging provide additional benefits due to their antioxidant characteristics. CDs are good at scavenging free radicals, improving the oxidative stability of packaged foods. Furthermore, CDs are biocompatible, non-toxic, and environmentally friendly, making them a safer option than synthetic antioxidants. Their multifunctionality, which includes antibacterial activity and photoluminescence, gives additional potential advantages, such as real-time monitoring of food quality.

Antimicrobial activity is an essential feature utilized in various active food packaging technologies. An evaluation was carried out on the effectiveness of CDs-based composite films (TPCD3) against food-borne pathogenic bacteria L. monocytogenes and E. coli, as shown in Fig. 7c,d. Chitosan exhibited antibacterial activity, suggesting a significant bacteriostatic effect because of the natural antimicrobial properties of CDs unloaded hydrogel. The addition of CDs to the polymer significantly improved antibacterial efficacy against E. coli and L. monocytogenes, as shown in Fig. 7c and (d). Freshly cultured E. coli and L. monocytogenes cells were separately exposed to the polymeric films and placed in an incubator at 37 °C with continuous shaking. After the incubation period, 100 μL of the culture was diluted and evenly spread over the nutrient agar plate. The plate was then incubated at 37 °C for 24 hours in static condition. When examining the bacterial cells incubated with the TPCD3 film it was observed that these films are highly effective in preventing bacterial growth. In contrast, the control plates for both types of bacteria clearly demonstrate significant bacterial growth. The results are in line with previous studies that show polymeric films containing doped quantum dots have powerful antibacterial effects against L. monocytogenes and E. coli. Travlou et al. investigated the antibacterial activity of sulfur and nitrogen-co-doped CDs against E. coli and B. subtilis [65]. They revealed that the physicochemical parameters and nanosize of CDs influence their antibacterial capabilities. The presence of the amide bond and amino group on the surface of CDs significantly impacts antibacterial activity. The positively charged form of this group interacts electrostatically with the phospholipid in the bacterial somatic membrane, causing cell death. The main N-species in our case are expected to be protonated, as demonstrated by the positive zeta potential of the CDs. Furthermore, the surface functional groups of reactive oxygen species/superoxide ions are activated to offer an antimicrobial effect. Reactive oxygen species, including O2 –, OH–, and HO2 –, are known to disrupt cellular components, such as nucleotides, lipids, proteins, and metabolites, ultimately resulting in cell death [65,66]. The binder of CDs, thermoplastic chitosan also demonstrate notable antibacterial effects, mainly attributed to the distinctive characteristics of chitosan, a natural polymer derived from chitin. The antibacterial mechanisms involve the rupture of bacterial cell membranes by interacting with the positively charged amino groups of chitosan and the negatively charged components of bacterial cell walls. This interaction results in damage to the cell membrane and the release of intracellular contents. In addition, chitosan can bind to essential metal ions, which can hinder the activity of bacterial enzymes and DNA replication. These films also serve as effective barriers that prevent bacterial adhesion and the formation of biofilms. This approach efficiently inhibits both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, making it suitable for use in food packaging, and surface coatings to prevent the growth of microorganisms [67].

Incorporating antibacterial compounds are essential in food packaging because they improve food safety by inhibiting the growth of harmful bacteria, hence lowering the risk of foodborne illness. They increase the shelf life of food goods by inhibiting bacterial development, which aids in maintaining nutritional quality and reducing food waste. These materials allow for decreasing chemical preservatives, which aligns with consumer demands for natural and organic products. Furthermore, antimicrobial packaging increases consumer confidence, provides market distinctiveness, and helps in compliance with strict food safety requirements. Overall, antibacterial materials help to make food safer, more durable, and of greater quality, which benefits both customers and the environment. CDs are better than metal oxide-based antibacterial compounds in terms of biocompatibility, less toxicity, and excellent environmental safety, rendering them more appropriate for food-related applications. CDs, unlike metal oxides, do not offer toxicity hazards or environmental concerns. Their ease of synthesis, cost-effectiveness, and potential for eco-friendly degradation further enhance their desirability as improved antibacterial materials for food packaging.

We were motivated to explore the potential use of CDs loaded hydrogels in food preserving due to their antibacterial properties and antioxidant characteristics. The hydrogel films produced have high transparency, antibacterial, and antioxidant properties, making them suitable for prolonging the shelf life of food products. Hydrogel films were utilised to encase bananas, and images were taken periodically throughout the storage period ( Fig. 8). On the first day, bananas were golden yellow and had flawless exteriors, as shown in Fig. 8. However, over the following days in storage, they ripened and eventually decayed. This is illustrated by the detection of shaded areas and marks. The banana covered with hydrogel containing CDs showed less decay than the banana without the coating. The study shows that the composite hydrogel film has strong antioxidant properties, which could improve fruit preservation and prolong its shelf life.

The effectiveness of food packaging relies mostly on the additive's capacity to detach from the packaging materials. Multiple research investigating the release characteristics of nanoparticles have consistently demonstrated that the water solubility of the polymer matrix, and dispersion of nanoparticles in hydrated environments all have a significant impact on the release properties of nanoparticles [68]. The Fig. 9(a−c) illustrates the release patterns of varied CDs concentrations from the TPC matrix when subjected to various food simulants. The profiles exhibited a gradual release within the first hour; perhaps attributed to the fact that chitosan is not soluble in water. The CD's release profile exhibited a plateau after 2 h and thereafter decelerated after 2.5 h. The rate of release in the alcohol solution was notably lower compared to that in water and 10 % ethanol. Specifically, the release rate decreased as the concentration of alcohol increased, in accordance with the alcohol content. The TPC films with a higher CDs concentration in water and 10 % ethanol had the maximum release rate. On the other hand, the TPC film with a low CD concentration in 50 % and 95 % ethanol had the lowest CD release. The total amount of CD released was considerably more in water and 10 % ethanol compared to 50 % and 95 % ethanol. This difference can be mainly attributed to the hydrophilicity and water solubility of CDs. The rate at which the release occurs is determined by the solubility of the polymer matrix and the solvent's affinity for the CDs. Furthermore, due to the hydrophilic nature of the CDs, the matrix readily absorbs aqueous solutions, which contain sufficient moisture to create a more porous structure. This, in turn, promotes the rapid release of the CDs. Nevertheless, alcohols are incapable of forming interactions with TPC polymers, leading to the entrapment of CDs within the matrix and therefore reducing diffusion and mobility within enclosed structures. Unlike alcoholic solutions, the CD was consequently released more rapidly and in greater quantities in water [69]. The encapsulation efficiency of the films was also evaluated by measuring their CDs remaining content. The encapsulation is directly related to the polarity and gradient of the solvents. Water has the lowest encapsulation efficiency due to easy release of CDs whereas with increasing the ethanol content, the encapsulation efficiency was enhanced (Fig. 9d). Moreover, CDs content into TPC films also showed significant changes (p < 0.05) in encapsulation efficiency. Basically CDs are prone to anchor the pendant functionalities of the TPC macrochains resulting better molecular secondary interactions (H-bonding, polar-polar). This secondary interaction affects the tight network and restricts the release of CDs. To ensure the cytotoxicity of the CDs loaded polymer films (TPCDs) MTT assay was performed for all the CDs reinforced films (TPCDs) and the results have been shown in Fig. 9(e). The data were tallied against the controlled system which could infer that all the TPCDs films showed more than 95 % cell viability even after 5 days of study. For TPCD1 there were no significant changes noticed compared to the control. With increasing the CDs, the cell viability is little decreased but after 5 days of incubation the TPCD3 showed ∼95 % of cell viability. This could imply the non-cytotoxic behavior of the CDs loaded polymer films. Several articles also reported similar kind of tests in ethanol water mixture which was used as a simulated food storage material. Gelatine based carbon dots functionalized film was reported where similar kind of study was reported where hydrophilic CDs release was monitored against ethanol water mixture [70]. Starch based blend film was also studied where more than 3 h release kinetics of CDs was reported [33]. Das et al. reported thermoplastic starch based CDs loaded functional film for CDs release test against ethanolic food simulants [14].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ethylene and CO2 production
ce:section.ce:para: The banana fruit, which was treated with ethylene in commercial facilities, had a characteristic ripening pattern when stored at a temperature of 20°C and a relative humidity of 80–85 %. There were noticeable variations in the production of ethylene and carbon dioxide in banana fruit that were treated with ethylene and had ripening initiated. These changes were observed across the evaluation days. The production of ethylene showed a steady increase starting from day 0 and reached its highest level on day 2, with an initial maximum of 0.56 ng/kg/s. Subsequently, ethylene production remained constant until day 8 and then had a significant surge during subsequent growth. The generation of carbon dioxide peaked at a maximum value of 30.92 µg/kg/s on day 4. The production rates were rather stable throughout the ripening and over-ripening period, as shown in Fig. 10.

In this study, we explore the creation of a thermoplastic chitosan film that is enhanced with carbon dots. This is achieved through a simple and efficient wet chemical blending technique. The synthesis of carbon dots was confirmed by analyzing their UV, PL, XPS, and FTIR characteristics. We conducted a thorough investigation and successfully demonstrated the water absorption characteristics of the TPC films. The hybrid films made with TPC displayed strong and flexible properties when subjected to uniaxial tension with ∼20 MPa of tensile strength after modification. In addition, the electron microscopic analysis confirmed the permeable characteristics where the OTR and WVTR of the hybrid film were measured to be ∼38.7 cm3.μm/(m2.d.kPa) and 633.8 g/m2.24 h, respectively. The hybrid films exhibited antibacterial properties attributed to the presence of antibacterial carbon dots. The TPC films exhibited limited transmission of UVA and UVB radiation, and this effect became more pronounced as the concentration of CDs in the TPC matrices increased. The antioxidant properties of the TPC films are confirmed by studying their radical scavenging behaviour. It is observed that the TPC films impregnated with CDs show antioxidant effects more than 80 % in both the DPPH and ABTS experiments. The presence of CDs enhances the antioxidant activity. Finally, for practical purposes, bananas are coated with CDs that contain hydrogel thin films. This coating allows the bananas to maintain their appearance for at least 5 days without deteriorating. These test results have the potential to position this material as an excellent alternative in the food sector and packaging industry.
